item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really liked the concept of this SCP. The execution was done fairly well, and I also enjoyed reading the tests with the D-Class subjects, intrigued at the different results from what they thought about at that moment. This one gets an upvote from me.

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Timer Expired. Edits made as discussed below. Author may revert at any time.

The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team

+ Show Block
- Hide Block

Thats a pretty neat skip. While I was reading it, I was strongly reminded of SCP-028, especially in how both anomalies cause the instant acquisition of knowledge. It seems possible that an SCP researcher might make that comparison in the documentation. As such, I am recommending the following edits:

Changing this:

SCP-1066 was recovered from the campus of U██ on ██/██/20██. The administration had been offering an "accelerated learning" program to students, for the standard four-year tuition plus a fee of $███████. It is unknown why nobody noticed the quick aging of some graduates; if this is a property of the object, it is yet to be discovered. The object was retrieved without casualties and Class-B amnestics were administered to the administration and all recorded graduates. It is very likely that some or most of the graduates were not recorded, but the risk of any graduates revealing the existence of SCP-1066 is low, and as such finding them is a low-priority task.

To this:

SCP-1066 was recovered from the campus of U██ on ██/██/20██. The administration had been offering an "accelerated learning" program to students, for the standard four-year tuition plus a fee of $███████. It is unknown why nobody noticed the quick aging of some graduates; if this is a property of the object, it is yet to be discovered. The object was retrieved without casualties and Class-B amnestics were administered to the administration and all recorded graduates. It is very likely that some or most of the graduates were not recorded, but the risk of any graduates revealing the existence of SCP-1066 is low, and as such finding them is a low-priority task. Similarities between the effects of SCP-1066 and SCP-028 have been noted, and investigation into a common origin, or whether one anomaly is an altered version of the other, is ongoing.

If anyone has other/better examples that could be used here please leave a comment saying so!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Interesting idea, although you could improve language.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Research Assistant ███ returned with a degree in "SCP Studies". The "SCP" in this case apparently is an acronym for "Super-Computer Processors". He now has a knowledge of super-computers consistent with the year 19██.

This piece was funny yet serious, it's kind of hard to pull off but hilarious when you do it right. Upvoted

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The phrasing of the sentence "earned a degree in whatever subject he was thinking of while signing the document, whether U██ offered classes in that subject…" raises a rather interesting question.

Can the signer gain a degree in ANY subject? Could they, for instance, gain a 4 year degree in an individual, say a 4 year degree in Will Smith?

I ask as the base of a hypothesis that what we have here is a rather useful tool for understanding the more dangerous or unknown SCPs. A 4 year course on SCP-689 for instance may gain the user knew knowledge on the creature's origin or abilities or even some way to better contain it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Nice idea, which might be even improved by a longer and more interesting experiment log. Also, the last test was very cool. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Have the 100th +1 :D
Really interesting, I would love to see some more testing results.

item.title: Image Attribution
item.content:encoded: The image in this article is derived from a CC image from Wikimedia Commons.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The last test was great. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I am under the impression that they have only one diploma, and reuse it for the tests.

Btw, upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The part about termination was also completely unessisary.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's based on what was known in 19██. I added a line to one of the tests to clarify that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Changed that section, becuase it's been bugging me ever since you pointed that out.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The decimal point+two figures really grates me. They really had over 10,000 people use this thing?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'd like to see more information on what the students actually learn, or whether they remember professors who really exist/existed. Do the professors remember the student?

Are the diplomas reusable? What happens if white-out is applied to a name and a new name scribbled in?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So "students" are educated on the course of their choice, even if U██ did not offer the course in the year 19██? What exactly do students learn? Is the knowledge they absorb based on what was known in the year 19██, or do they receive a "modern" education? I laughed at the last guy who ended up with a 19██-era level of knowledge on supercomputers (depending on when 19██ really is, he may have just wasted four years learning stuff that is no longer relevant).

Anyhow, I liked the article. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If you're trying to imply that something really bad happened at the university back then, I think a decently high death rate works actually.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The date on the diploma is blacked out in the article, but is still in the picture as Roman numerals. Just thought I'd point that out, I really like this SCP by the way.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There's a point there now. Thanks for noticing that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Subject returned dead.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It is worth noting that about █% of subjects do not survive the four years. In these cases, their corpse will return with the expected amount of decay. The death rate is consistent with that of students at U██ in 19██.

Wait, at least 1% of students who went to that university died?

When a man who is at least 17 years of age signs his name in the space labeled "Graduating Student", he will disappear for 4-10 minutes and return four years older. The subject seems to have lived those years as a student at U██ in 19██, and earned a degree in whatever subject he was thinking of while signing the document, whether U██ offered classes in that subject in 19██ or not.

So how long before the foundation decides to try to get an agent/researcher a degree in "killing 682-ology"?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, I pretty much would have upvoted for the number alone, but I like this on its own merits. And I imagined this University being more like a British school/college from the 70s instead of what I've been writing about, so it stands on its own.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I… I love this. The memetic ones made me go "HAH!".

Upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Actually, before I edited it significantly there were much worse hazards. Maiming was a double-digit percent. Now it's more like a simulation of the four years.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: the "hazards", in this case, are kegstands

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think it's justified. It suggests that this thing is simulating the entire 4 years, including any of the hazards that would be involved along the way.

This is a rad SCP. The "knowledge of super-computers consistent with the year 19██" thing cracked me up, and the rest of the addendum's examples explain the effects/possibilities well. Nice work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I liked this a lot, but when I looked at the recently created page I was expecting the battle of hastings.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I changed the university to something less likely to be confused with ███████ University. And removed the completely unnecessary maiming.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I believe Eskobar has a few articles involving ████████ University. I don't know if the concept is his or not, but the ones I know of are SCP-961, SCP-877 and SCP-1080.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I remember reading about that, but i forget where. Would you mind linking me?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Personally, I think I would prefer the SCP without the random deaths and injuries. It seems the losing 4 years is really enough to counterweight the potential advantages gained.
Not all SCPs have to be unnecessarily harmful- just look at Mr. Deeds, or 914.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Honestly, I really want this to be ████████ University.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: while I very much like this, I have one mild note to make, im not sure how you could fix this but when I first read this i mistook ████ ███ University for the Group of Interest known as "████████ University" or is it just me?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Though I may be misinterpreting, I'm a bit tickled by the idea that, following a 4 year study of memetics, the guy was unwilling to so much as speak with anyone else. But then, that might be the only reasonable reaction…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks. Those sections were left over from a draft or two ago.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The sections describing what the Ds were imprisoned for are completely irrelevant as far as I can tell. Get rid of them.

Additionally, "left chest" = "left pectoral".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Reminds me of SCP-028 a lot, but the feel's definitely unique enough to work on its own. Nice job for a first, but I'm with Soulless - injuries/squick usually isn't added unless it's critical to the SCP itself. I know this SCP draws from that aspect as a bit of a "Monkey Paw Logic," but it'd probably be okay to tone it down just a bit.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I spent some time thinking about that. I eventually decided to give them no memory of anything not directly related to their degree, but I still think that it was kind of a cop-out. The deaths were in fact tacked on to make it less desirable. The whole idea is that something bad happened at the university during those four years. But I had trouble thinking of what and some research didn't help, so I just stuck a [DATA EXPUNGED] on there.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It reminds me of [REDACTED] University. It's rather interesting though, albeit the random injuries/deaths doesn't much help.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is somewhat interesting. What happens if they try to leave the campus or otherwise not pursue a degree? I don't like the random deaths bit, that seems a bit tacked on to make this less desirable. It almost works, actually.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Post this in the announcement thread.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: New SCP. And my first.
